title: RAII and deterministic cleanup

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for RAII and deterministic cleanup; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to RAII and deterministic cleanup
sources.claim: RAII binds resource ownership to object lifetime: construction acquires or establishes ownership and deterministic destruction releases it during every normal scope exit and stack unwind.

canonicalExplanation.summary: RAII binds resource ownership to object lifetime: construction acquires or establishes ownership and deterministic destruction releases it during every normal scope exit and stack unwind.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An ifstream closes automatically when its scope ends, including when a later operation throws an exception. Your goal is to refactor manual cleanup into an exception-safe RAII design. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and initialization; Value, reference and const-reference parameters. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: RAII binds resource ownership to object lifetime: construction acquires or establishes ownership and deterministic destruction releases it during every normal scope exit and stack unwind. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: RAII does not resolve ownership cycles, process termination can skip normal destruction, and constructors must handle partial acquisition with member RAII. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: RAII is only about memory allocated with new and does not apply to files, locks, sockets, or transactions.

includes: Acquire resources in constructors and release them in destructors or standard wrappers.
Refactor manual cleanup into an exception-safe RAII design.
excludes: RAII does not resolve ownership cycles, process termination can skip normal destruction, and constructors must handle partial acquisition with member RAII.

outcomes: Refactor manual cleanup into an exception-safe RAII design.

examples.title: RAII and deterministic cleanup: defined case
examples.situation: An ifstream closes automatically when its scope ends, including when a later operation throws an exception.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An ifstream closes automatically when its scope ends, including when a later operation throws an exception.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: RAII applies to any resource with scoped ownership and release, including memory, files, locks, handles, and rollback guards.
examples.result: The defined result follows because RAII applies to any resource with scoped ownership and release, including memory, files, locks, handles, and rollback guards.

examples.title: RAII and deterministic cleanup: boundary case
examples.situation: A lock_guard releases its mutex at block exit rather than relying on every return path to call unlock manually.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A lock_guard releases its mutex at block exit rather than relying on every return path to call unlock manually.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: RAII does not resolve ownership cycles, process termination can skip normal destruction, and constructors must handle partial acquisition with member RAII.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: RAII is only about memory allocated with new and does not apply to files, locks, sockets, or transactions.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Refactor manual cleanup into an exception-safe RAII design.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: RAII is only about memory allocated with new and does not apply to files, locks, sockets, or transactions.
misconceptions.correction: RAII applies to any resource with scoped ownership and release, including memory, files, locks, handles, and rollback guards.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: RAII is only about memory allocated with new and does not apply to files, locks, sockets, or transactions.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model RAII and deterministic cleanup: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
RAII does not resolve ownership cycles, process termination can skip normal destruction, and constructors must handle partial acquisition with member RAII.

recap.summary: You should now be able to refactor manual cleanup into an exception-safe RAII design. Keep this boundary in view: RAII does not resolve ownership cycles, process termination can skip normal destruction, and constructors must handle partial acquisition with member RAII.
